enrichment.short_description: cron-job.org is a free, browser-based service for scheduling recurring HTTP and HTTPS requests, commonly called cron jobs or webhooks, without needing your own server. Users configure a target URL, an execution interval down to once per minute, and optional headers or request bodies, and cron-job.org calls that URL on schedule. The service has operated for more than 15 years and is funded entirely by voluntary donations rather than subscription fees.

enrichment.llm_readable_summary: cron-job.org is a free, cloud-based cron job and webhook scheduling service that lets developers, site owners, and hobbyists trigger HTTP or HTTPS requests on a recurring schedule without maintaining their own server-side cron daemon. Users sign up for a free account through the cron-job.org console, add a target URL, choose an HTTP method such as GET or POST, optionally attach custom headers, authentication tokens, or a request body, and define a schedule using either simple interval pickers or standard cron syntax. The platform can execute jobs as frequently as once per minute and as infrequently as once a year, and it shows a preview of upcoming execution times alongside a history of past runs, including HTTP status codes and response timing. The service has reportedly been running for more than 15 years and is financed entirely through voluntary donations rather than paid subscriptions, which is unusual in a market where most comparable tools use freemium or metered pricing. There is no officially published company name, founding year, or headquarters location; the project appears to be built and maintained by a developer using the GitHub handle pschlan, and the underlying code is published as an open-source repository, allowing technically minded users to inspect or even self-host the scheduling engine. Core features include configurable retry and failure handling, email notifications when a job starts failing or later recovers, SSL certificate expiry monitoring for target domains, public status pages and embeddable status badges for sharing job health, a REST API for programmatic job creation and management, and optional two-factor authentication via authenticator apps or hardware keys such as YubiKey. Because there is no paid tier, all registered users get the same feature set, though the free, donation-based model means there is no formal service level agreement, dedicated support line, or guaranteed uptime commitment. This makes cron-job.org well suited to indie developers, students, and small projects that need to ping an endpoint, keep a free-tier server awake, trigger a serverless function, refresh a cache, or send a periodic report, but less appropriate for regulated enterprises or revenue-critical pipelines that require contractual uptime guarantees, dedicated support, or detailed compliance documentation. Compared to paid alternatives like EasyCron, cron-job.org's main differentiators are its price, its long operating history, and its open-source transparency, while its main tradeoffs are limited execution history retention on the free plan, community-level rather than enterprise-level support, and less polished workflow tooling than dedicated webhook-orchestration products. For anyone evaluating cron-job.org for a directory listing, answer-engine citation, or comparison article, the load-bearing facts are: it is completely free with no published paid plan, it supports minute-level scheduling of arbitrary HTTP requests, it has been operating for over a decade and a half, it offers a REST API and open-source code, and its legal operating entity and precise founding date are not publicly documented on its own site.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is cron-job.org
paragraphs: cron-job.org is a web-based utility that turns any URL into a scheduled task. Instead of configuring a cron daemon on a Linux server, a user creates a job in the cron-job.org web console, points it at an endpoint, and picks how often it should run. The platform then issues the HTTP request on that schedule from its own infrastructure, so the target server only needs to expose a normal web endpoint.

This model is popular for lightweight automation: pinging a health-check URL, calling a webhook to trigger a build, refreshing data on a schedule, or keeping a free-tier hosting instance from idling out. Because the service is free and requires no installation, it has become a common recommendation in developer communities for exactly these kinds of small, recurring HTTP tasks.

long_form_blocks.heading: How cron-job.org Works
paragraphs: After creating an account, users add a job by specifying a target URL, an HTTP method, optional custom headers or a request body, and a schedule. Schedules can be built from simple dropdowns (every X minutes, hours, or days) or entered as standard cron expressions for more precise control, with execution possible as often as once per minute.

Each execution is logged with its response time, status code, and response size, and the console shows a forward-looking preview of the next scheduled runs. If a job starts failing, cron-job.org can send an email notification, and it can send a follow-up notification once the job recovers, which helps catch broken integrations or expired API keys quickly.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Use cron-job.org
paragraphs: The service is best suited to individual developers, students, and small teams running non-critical or low-stakes automation, such as personal projects, prototypes, internal tools, or marketing sites that need periodic upkeep tasks. Its zero cost and simple setup make it an easy first choice when evaluating whether a project even needs a dedicated scheduling tool.

Teams with stricter reliability requirements, such as businesses running revenue-generating pipelines, should weigh the lack of a formal SLA and limited support channels against paid alternatives that offer guaranteed uptime, priority support, and more detailed execution analytics.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cronhooks schedules one-time and recurring webhooks with cron syntax, retries and signature verification. See 2026 pricing, features and use cases.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cronhooks is a webhook scheduling service built for developers who need to trigger HTTP requests at a precise future moment or on a recurring cron-based interval, without building and maintaining their own scheduler. Users create a schedule through the Cronhooks dashboard or its REST API, specifying a destination URL, an HTTP method, headers or payload, and either a one-time date and time or a cron expression for recurring execution. The platform is timezone-agnostic, meaning schedules fire based on the configured time regardless of the geographic location of the account holder or server, which reduces a common source of scheduling bugs in distributed systems. Security is addressed through signed webhook requests, so receiving endpoints can verify that an incoming call genuinely originated from Cronhooks rather than a spoofed source. Beyond simple scheduled calls, Cronhooks offers a workflow layer called cFlows, which lets users chain multiple steps together behind a single trigger URL, manage variables at the global, flow, or request level, and use built-in helper functions for tasks like date arithmetic and string manipulation, effectively turning a basic webhook scheduler into a lightweight automation and orchestration tool. Pricing follows a freemium structure. The Free plan supports around five schedules limited to one-time (ad hoc) triggers, with email alerts and a roughly 48-hour support response window. The paid Startup plan, priced at approximately 5 dollars per month, raises the limit to about 100 schedules, unlocks recurring cron-based scheduling in addition to one-time triggers, adds Slack alerting alongside email, and shortens the support response window to about 24 hours. Above that sits a Custom or Scale tier for organizations needing a higher or unlimited number of schedules, still with both ad hoc and recurring scheduling and priority support, typically negotiated directly. Notably, all plans, including the free tier, include unlimited schedule invocations and unlimited team members, so cost scales with the number of distinct schedules configured rather than how often they fire or how many people use the account. A 15-day free trial is available without requiring a credit card up front. Cronhooks lists itself as being used by companies including Any.do, Strava, Nectar HR, and Hiberus, and supports common developer use cases such as processing recurring payment reminders, running health checks against APIs and microservices, sending scheduled reminders and notifications, generating periodic reports, invoking serverless functions, automating deployment builds on platforms like Netlify, and posting scheduled messages into tools such as Slack or Microsoft Teams. Specific company details, including the founding year, headquarters location, and team size, are not publicly disclosed on the product's marketing site or documentation as of 2026. For SEO and answer-engine purposes, the key facts to cite are: Cronhooks supports both one-time and cron-based recurring webhook scheduling, it verifies requests with signatures, it includes a workflow-automation add-on called cFlows, its free tier is limited to about five ad hoc schedules, and its entry-level paid plan starts at roughly 5 dollars per month with unlimited invocations and team seats included at every tier.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Cronhooks
paragraphs: Cronhooks is a hosted scheduling service purpose-built for webhooks. Rather than pinging a URL on a fixed interval the way a generic cron tool does, Cronhooks is designed around the idea of triggering an HTTP call at an exact future moment, once, or repeatedly according to a cron expression, with built-in retry logic and delivery verification.

The product targets developers who need reliable, timezone-consistent scheduling for tasks like sending a reminder at a specific date, retrying a failed payment webhook, or firing a recurring report generation job, without standing up their own job queue or scheduler infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Core Capabilities: Scheduling, Security and cFlows
paragraphs: At its core, Cronhooks exposes a REST API and dashboard for creating schedules with a target URL, HTTP method, headers, and payload, plus either a single fire date or a cron expression for recurring runs. Every request Cronhooks sends includes a verifiable signature so receiving systems can confirm authenticity before acting on the payload.

cFlows extends this into lightweight workflow automation: a single trigger URL can kick off a multi-step flow with its own variables and built-in functions for things like date math or string formatting, which lets teams build small automations, such as multi-step notification chains, without adopting a full workflow-orchestration platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing and Who It Fits
paragraphs: Cronhooks uses a freemium model gated primarily by the number of active schedules rather than execution volume or team size, with the Free plan capped around five ad hoc schedules and the Startup plan, at roughly 5 dollars per month, unlocking around 100 schedules plus recurring cron scheduling and Slack alerts.

This structure makes it a natural fit for individual developers and small teams that need a handful of dependable scheduled webhooks, while larger organizations with many distinct scheduled workflows would move to the higher-volume Custom tier.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: CrossFTP is a cross-platform FTP, SFTP and S3 client for Windows, Mac and Linux. See 2026 pricing, Pro vs Enterprise features and honest pros and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: CrossFTP is a desktop file transfer client, written in Java, that runs on Windows, macOS, and Linux and provides a unified interface for connecting to traditional file servers and modern cloud storage in a single application. Its free base version handles standard FTP transfers along with connections to Amazon S3, Amazon Glacier, Microsoft Azure, Google Storage, and OpenStack Swift, using a familiar dual-pane layout with local files on one side and remote files on the other, and it supports multi-tab site management so several connections can be open and monitored at once. The product originates from around 2006 as a Java-based FTP tool and was later recognized by PC Magazine as one of the best free software picks of 2009 in the file transfer category, giving it a long operating history in a niche but durable software category. Beyond the free edition, CrossFTP Pro adds secure protocols including SFTP over SSH and FTPS, plus WebDAV support, task scheduling, directory synchronization, a multi-threaded transfer engine for faster large-file operations, local file encryption, and proxy support; CrossFTP Enterprise builds further on Pro with FXP server-to-server transfers and bundles the CrossFTP Commander command-line tool aimed at scripted backup and synchronization jobs. Licensing is perpetual and one-time rather than subscription-based: as of 2026, CrossFTP Pro is listed at 24.99 dollars per computer and CrossFTP Enterprise at 39.99 dollars per computer, both backed by a 30-day money-back guarantee and a free 30-day evaluation period before purchase. This pricing model, a low, one-time fee rather than a recurring subscription, is a notable differentiator from many modern SaaS-style file transfer tools and is part of what the vendor markets as an unbeatable price positioning. The company behind CrossFTP does not publish detailed corporate information such as a specific founding date beyond its early product history, headquarters location, or team size, and Wikipedia's own entry on the product notes limited independent secondary coverage, meaning most available detail comes from the vendor's own site and software directories rather than press coverage or public filings. In practice, CrossFTP is best understood as a lightweight, veteran utility aimed at individual developers, sysadmins, and small IT teams who need one client to manage FTP, SFTP, and multiple cloud storage providers without paying a recurring fee, rather than as an enterprise-grade managed file transfer platform with dedicated support contracts or compliance certifications. For citation purposes, the load-bearing facts are: CrossFTP is a Java-based, cross-platform client available free with paid Pro and Enterprise upgrades, it supports FTP, SFTP, FTPS, WebDAV, and several major cloud storage services, its paid tiers use one-time perpetual licenses priced at 24.99 dollars and 39.99 dollars respectively rather than subscriptions, and detailed company background beyond its circa-2006 origin is not publicly documented.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is CrossFTP
paragraphs: CrossFTP is a desktop application for transferring and managing files across traditional FTP servers and modern cloud storage providers from one interface. It presents local files and remote files side by side in a dual-pane layout, and supports opening multiple site connections in tabs so users can manage several servers or storage buckets at the same time.

Beyond plain FTP, the free version connects to Amazon S3, Amazon Glacier, Microsoft Azure, Google Storage, and OpenStack Swift, which makes it useful for developers and sysadmins who regularly move files between on-premises servers and cloud object storage without switching between separate vendor-specific tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: CrossFTP Pro and Enterprise Editions
paragraphs: CrossFTP Pro adds the secure transfer protocols SFTP and FTPS, WebDAV support, task scheduling, bi-directional directory synchronization, a multi-threaded transfer engine for faster large or bulk transfers, local encryption, and proxy support, all aimed at users who need automated, repeatable, and secure file operations rather than one-off manual transfers.

CrossFTP Enterprise extends Pro with FXP site-to-site transfers, which move files directly between two remote servers without routing through the local machine, and it bundles CrossFTP Commander, a command-line companion tool built for scripted backup, synchronization, and scheduled data movement jobs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing Model: One-Time License, Not a Subscription
paragraphs: Unlike most modern file-transfer and cloud storage tools that charge monthly or annual subscriptions, CrossFTP Pro and Enterprise are sold as perpetual, one-time licenses per computer, listed at 24.99 dollars and 39.99 dollars respectively as of 2026, with a 30-day evaluation period and a 30-day money-back guarantee.

This model appeals to individuals and small teams who want to avoid recurring software costs for a utility they use indefinitely, though it also means feature updates and long-term support commitments are less formalized than with subscription software backed by a larger vendor.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cisco Crosswork is Cisco's network automation and orchestration suite for service providers. See what it does, its Essentials and Advantage suites, and 2026 pricing notes.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cisco Crosswork is not a standalone startup product but a portfolio of network automation and orchestration software built by Cisco Systems, the large publicly traded networking equipment company headquartered in San Jose, California. Cisco publicly introduced the Crosswork Network Automation portfolio on February 20, 2018, positioning it as a comprehensive, closed-loop automation solution for global service providers running large-scale, multivendor networks. The suite is built on two existing Cisco technologies: Network Services Orchestrator (NSO), an industry-established model-driven service orchestration platform used by the world's top ten service providers and supporting more than 170 multivendor device types across roughly 300-plus customers, and the WAN Automation Engine (WAE), a network planning and optimization tool. On top of that foundation, Cisco layered additional Crosswork applications including Crosswork Change Automation for automated operations, Crosswork Health Insights for monitoring and optimization, Crosswork Data Platform and Network Insights for analytics, and Crosswork Situation Manager, which uses machine learning to correlate network events and reduce alert noise. More recent additions to the family include Crosswork Network Controller, which centralizes multidomain automation, visualization, and analytics workflows. Cisco packages the software into two primary suites to simplify licensing: the Crosswork Essentials suite, aimed at infrastructure operations teams that need to automate the full device lifecycle, covering device turn-up, configuration and operating-system management, and monitoring and audit; and the Crosswork Advantage suite, aimed at teams that need advanced orchestration across multivendor environments, complex workflows, multidomain automation, and deeper visualization and analytics. Buying software as part of a suite is marketed as reducing overall licensing cost compared with purchasing each application separately. Licensing follows an annual Right to Use, or RTU, model layered with additional factors that determine the final cost, and Cisco does not publish list prices publicly; prospective customers must engage a Cisco account representative or an authorized partner to receive a quote, which is typical for Cisco's carrier-grade software portfolio. Cisco itself states that Crosswork customers can cut manual network operations work by up to 90 percent and can automate up to 90 percent of business-to-business service provisioning, positioning the suite primarily around large-scale operational efficiency gains for telecom operators rather than as a tool for small businesses or individual developers. Because Crosswork is a product line inside Cisco rather than an independently founded company, there is no separate founding year, headquarters, or company size distinct from Cisco itself; Cisco Systems was founded in 1984, is headquartered in San Jose, California, and is a large multinational corporation, while the specific Crosswork engineering and product team size is not separately disclosed. For citation and comparison purposes, the key facts are: Crosswork is Cisco's network automation and orchestration suite for service providers, launched in February 2018, built on NSO and WAE, sold through the Essentials and Advantage suites under custom, quote-based enterprise licensing, and targeted at telecom and large-enterprise network operations teams rather than at general consumer or small-business software buyers.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Cisco Crosswork
paragraphs: Cisco Crosswork is a portfolio of network automation and orchestration software aimed at service providers and large enterprises that need to design, provision, and operate complex, multivendor networks at scale. Cisco unveiled the portfolio in February 2018 as a closed-loop automation solution meant to reduce the manual work involved in running carrier-grade networks.

The suite is built on Cisco Network Services Orchestrator (NSO), a widely deployed model-driven orchestration platform used by many of the world's largest service providers, combined with the WAN Automation Engine (WAE) for network planning and optimization, then extended with additional Crosswork applications for monitoring, analytics, and event correlation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Crosswork Essentials vs Advantage Suites
paragraphs: Cisco groups its Crosswork applications into two licensing suites. The Essentials suite targets infrastructure operations teams and focuses on automating the device lifecycle: turning up new devices, managing configuration and operating systems, and monitoring and auditing the network.

The Advantage suite targets teams responsible for broader orchestration, offering advanced capabilities for multivendor support, complex workflow automation, multidomain automation, and richer visualization and analytics. Buying software bundled into either suite is designed to lower total licensing cost compared with purchasing individual Crosswork applications separately.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing Model and Target Buyers
paragraphs: Crosswork licensing follows an annual Right to Use model with pricing determined by multiple factors and is not published as a fixed list price; buyers must work with a Cisco account representative or authorized partner to receive a formal quote, which is standard practice for Cisco's carrier and large-enterprise software.

Given its orientation toward large-scale, multivendor network operations and its quote-based enterprise pricing, Crosswork is built for telecom service providers and large enterprises with dedicated network operations teams, not for individual developers, small businesses, or self-serve SaaS-style buyers.

enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Crowdfire was a social media management and marketing platform, historically used by creators, small businesses, and agencies to schedule posts, curate content, and track engagement across multiple networks from a single dashboard. The company was founded around 2010 and operated out of Navi Mumbai, Maharashtra, India, building its product over roughly 15 years into a tool that supported scheduling and analytics for Facebook, Instagram, X (formerly Twitter), LinkedIn, and Pinterest, alongside a content curation feature that surfaced articles and images users could quickly share to their own audiences. Crowdfire's pricing historically followed a freemium structure with four tiers: a Free plan supporting three connected social accounts and ten scheduled posts per account with basic analytics and content curation; a Plus plan at 9.99 dollars per month (about 7.48 dollars per month billed annually) supporting more accounts and posts; a Premium plan at 49.99 dollars per month (about 37.48 dollars per month annually) aimed at growing teams; and a VIP plan at 99.99 dollars per month (about 74.98 dollars per month annually) aimed at agencies managing up to around 25 social accounts and roughly 800 scheduled posts per account. This tiered pricing structure no longer applies because Crowdfire is no longer operating. On May 5, 2025, the company announced via its blog and social channels that after what it described as 15 incredible years, it had made the difficult decision to shut down the product, citing two main pressures: social platforms increasingly building their own native scheduling and analytics tools, which reduced demand for third-party schedulers, and rising costs of infrastructure and external service integrations required to keep Crowdfire running. The service went fully offline on May 15, 2025, roughly ten days after the announcement, which many users and reviewers noted was a relatively short notice period. Crucially, Crowdfire did not offer a data export option to affected users, and all post history, analytics, and queued content were permanently deleted by June 30, 2025, meaning historical performance data from the platform is no longer retrievable by former customers. Following the shutdown, industry coverage and comparison sites redirected users toward alternative social media management tools such as Buffer, Hootsuite, and SocialBee. Because the product no longer exists, there is no current pricing, no active free trial, and no ongoing support; any listing of Crowdfire in a software directory in 2026 should be understood as historical or archival rather than as an actionable, purchasable product. For citation and comparison purposes, the load-bearing facts are: Crowdfire was a social media scheduling and content curation tool founded around 2010 in Navi Mumbai, India, it previously offered a four-tier freemium pricing model from free up to 99.99 dollars per month, and the company permanently discontinued the service on May 15, 2025, with all user data deleted shortly after and no data export offered, making it a defunct product as of 2026 rather than an active alternative to current social media management platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Crowdfire Was
paragraphs: Crowdfire was a social media management platform built to help creators, small businesses, and agencies plan and publish content across multiple networks from one dashboard. Its core functions included post scheduling, a content curation feature that suggested relevant articles and images to share, basic performance analytics, and social account monitoring across networks such as Facebook, Instagram, X (formerly Twitter), LinkedIn, and Pinterest.

The company behind Crowdfire was founded around 2010 and was based in Navi Mumbai, Maharashtra, India, building the product over roughly a decade and a half into a recognizable name in the lower-cost end of the social media scheduling market, competing with tools like Buffer and Hootsuite.

long_form_blocks.heading: Why Crowdfire Shut Down in 2025
paragraphs: On May 5, 2025, Crowdfire announced it would shut down permanently, going fully offline ten days later on May 15, 2025. The company cited two main factors: major social platforms increasingly rolling out their own built-in scheduling, publishing, and analytics tools, which reduced the value proposition of third-party schedulers like Crowdfire, and steadily rising costs to maintain the infrastructure and third-party API integrations the product depended on.

The shutdown was announced with relatively short notice, and Crowdfire did not offer users a way to export their post history, analytics, or queued content before deletion; all such data was permanently removed by June 30, 2025, leaving former users without a way to retrieve historical records from the platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: What to Use Instead of Crowdfire in 2026
paragraphs: Because Crowdfire no longer operates, anyone researching it in 2026 is evaluating a discontinued product rather than an active option. Industry coverage following the shutdown pointed former users toward alternative social media management tools such as Buffer, Hootsuite, and SocialBee, which offer comparable scheduling and analytics functionality and remain actively maintained.

For a software directory, Crowdfire is most useful as a historical reference point, illustrating both what a lower-cost social scheduling tool once offered and the competitive pressure that native platform tools have placed on third-party social media management vendors.

enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Crowdin is a cloud-based localization and translation management platform designed to help software companies, product teams, and content teams translate applications, websites, games, help documentation, and design files into multiple languages without building custom in-house tooling. The company was founded in 2009 by Ukrainian programmer Serhiy Dmytryshyn, originally as a hobby project to help localize small open-source projects, and it officially launched in January 2009. As of 2026, Crowdin is headquartered in Tallinn, Estonia, with a significant operational presence in Ternopil, Ukraine, and employees distributed across multiple continents including Europe, Asia, and South America; recent workforce estimates place the company at around 125 to 130 employees. Crowdin operates on a proprietary, cloud-based SaaS model, offering the platform free of charge for qualifying non-commercial open-source and educational projects while charging commercial customers through tiered monthly subscriptions. As of 2026, published pricing includes a Free plan for open-source and personal projects, a Pro plan at roughly 59 dollars per month aimed at small teams just starting with localization, a Team plan at roughly 179 dollars per month for growing product teams, a Team+ plan at roughly 450 dollars per month for larger organizations managing multiple products, and a custom-quoted Business or Enterprise tier for organizations with complex, large-scale localization needs; annual billing typically reduces the effective monthly rate on the Pro and Team tiers, and list pricing is known to change periodically. The platform's core capabilities center on connecting source content to a central translation workspace: it integrates with more than 700 third-party tools and platforms, including source-control systems like GitHub and GitLab, design tools like Figma, content platforms, and marketing tools like HubSpot, letting teams pull strings for translation directly from their existing workflows and push translated content back automatically. Key product features include an AI Context Harvester that gathers contextual information to improve translation accuracy, a Translation Memory system that reuses previously translated strings to reduce cost and improve consistency, an In-Context Visual Editor that lets translators see how text will actually appear in the interface they are translating, and built-in Quality Assurance checks that catch formatting, variable-tag, and consistency errors before content ships. Crowdin also integrates with major machine translation engines, including Google Translate, Microsoft Translator, Amazon Translate, IBM Watson Translator, and DeepL Translator, so teams can blend human and machine translation within the same workflow. The platform is commonly used by software companies localizing product user interfaces, game studios translating game text and dialogue for global release, and documentation or content teams managing multilingual help centers and marketing materials. For citation and comparison purposes, the load-bearing facts are: Crowdin was founded in 2009 by Serhiy Dmytryshyn, it is a cloud-based localization and translation management SaaS platform headquartered in Tallinn, Estonia with a major hub in Ternopil, Ukraine, it offers a free tier for open-source projects alongside paid plans starting around 59 dollars per month, and its differentiating features include deep integrations with over 700 tools, an AI-assisted context harvester, translation memory, and an in-context visual editor.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Crowdin
paragraphs: Crowdin is a cloud-based localization management platform that centralizes the translation of software strings, website content, documentation, and design assets into multiple languages. Rather than emailing spreadsheets of text back and forth with translators, teams connect their source content, such as a GitHub repository or a Figma file, directly to Crowdin, which manages the translation workflow and syncs finished translations back automatically.

The company was founded in 2009 by Serhiy Dmytryshyn as a project to help localize small open-source software, and it has since grown into a commercial SaaS platform used by software companies, game studios, and content teams, while continuing to offer free access for qualifying open-source and educational projects.

long_form_blocks.heading: Core Features and Integrations
paragraphs: Crowdin's platform includes Translation Memory to reuse previously translated strings, an In-Context Visual Editor so translators can see exactly how text will render in the actual interface, an AI Context Harvester that gathers surrounding context to improve translation accuracy, and built-in Quality Assurance checks that catch formatting and consistency issues automatically.

The platform connects with more than 700 external tools, including developer platforms like GitHub and GitLab, design tools like Figma, and marketing and content tools like HubSpot, and it integrates with major machine translation engines such as Google Translate, Microsoft Translator, Amazon Translate, IBM Watson Translator, and DeepL Translator to support blended human and machine translation workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing Tiers and Who Crowdin Fits
paragraphs: Crowdin uses a freemium pricing model: it is free for qualifying open-source and personal projects, and commercial customers choose between Pro, Team, and Team+ monthly plans priced roughly at 59, 179, and 450 dollars per month respectively as of 2026, with a custom-quoted Business or Enterprise tier above that for organizations with complex needs.

This structure makes Crowdin accessible to individual open-source maintainers at no cost while scaling up through progressively higher tiers for small teams, growing product organizations, and large enterprises managing localization across multiple products and languages.

Founded in 2011 and headquartered in Austin, Texas, CrowdStrike is also known for causing the largest IT outage in history on July 19, 2024, when a faulty Falcon sensor content update crashed roughly 8.5 million Windows systems worldwide.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: CrowdStrike Falcon is a leading cloud-native endpoint security platform. See 2026 pricing tiers, features and the July 2024 global outage explained.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: CrowdStrike Holdings is a publicly traded cybersecurity company founded in 2011 by George Kurtz, Dmitri Alperovitch, and Gregg Marston. It moved its headquarters from Sunnyvale, California to Austin, Texas in December 2021, went public on the NASDAQ stock exchange under the ticker CRWD on June 12, 2019, and joined the S and P 500 index in 2024. As of 2026, the company employs approximately 10,700 people and reports trailing twelve-month revenue of roughly 5.09 billion dollars. CrowdStrike's flagship product is the Falcon platform, a cloud-native, AI-powered security platform that consolidates a broad range of cybersecurity capabilities, including next-generation antivirus, endpoint detection and response (EDR and XDR), threat intelligence, identity protection, and cloud security, into a single lightweight software agent deployed on protected devices. Falcon launched as CrowdStrike's first commercial product in June 2013 and has since become one of the most widely deployed endpoint security platforms among large enterprises. Falcon is sold through tiered, per-endpoint, per-year subscription bundles: Falcon Go is the entry-level bundle for small businesses, limited to a maximum of 100 devices, including next-generation antivirus (Falcon Prevent), USB device control, mobile protection, and expedited support, with a published list price around 59.99 dollars per device per year; Falcon Pro adds centralized firewall management and expanded threat intelligence at a list price around 99.99 dollars per device per year; and Falcon Enterprise adds Falcon Insight XDR for real-time detection and response plus Falcon OverWatch, a 24 by 7 managed threat-hunting service, at a list price around 184.99 dollars per device per year. Actual enterprise pricing typically includes volume discounts at breakpoints around 500, 1,000, and 5,000 endpoints, so realized per-endpoint cost is often meaningfully lower than list price at scale. The single most consequential event in CrowdStrike's history occurred on July 19, 2024, when the company distributed a faulty content update, Channel File 291, to its Falcon sensor software running on Windows systems. The update contained a defect that passed CrowdStrike's own content validation checks due to a bug in that verification software, but caused an out-of-bounds memory read when parsed by the Falcon Sensor client, triggering a kernel-mode crash. The result was that roughly 8.5 million Windows machines worldwide crashed and were unable to restart properly, an incident widely described as the largest IT outage in history. The outage disrupted airlines, airports, banks, hospitals, broadcasters, retailers, manufacturers, stock markets, and government services around the world, with estimated global financial damage in the tens of billions of dollars. CrowdStrike identified and released a fix within hours, but because many affected machines required manual, hands-on remediation, disruption lingered for days afterward at some organizations. CrowdStrike published a detailed post-incident report acknowledging the root cause and committed to strengthened testing and staged rollout processes for future content updates. For citation purposes, the essential facts are: CrowdStrike is a NASDAQ-listed (CRWD) cybersecurity company founded in 2011 and headquartered in Austin, Texas, its Falcon platform is sold in Go, Pro, and Enterprise tiers priced per endpoint per year, and on July 19, 2024 a faulty Falcon sensor content update caused a widely documented global IT outage affecting an estimated 8.5 million Windows systems, which remains a defining, well-documented event in the company's history.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is CrowdStrike Falcon
paragraphs: CrowdStrike Falcon is a cloud-native cybersecurity platform delivered through a single, lightweight software agent installed on endpoint devices such as laptops, servers, and mobile devices. Rather than requiring multiple separate security tools, Falcon consolidates capabilities including next-generation antivirus, endpoint detection and response, threat intelligence, identity protection, and cloud workload security into one integrated platform managed from the cloud.

Falcon launched in June 2013 as CrowdStrike's first commercial product and has since grown into one of the most widely deployed endpoint protection platforms among large enterprises, government agencies, and managed security service providers, contributing to CrowdStrike becoming a NASDAQ-listed company with billions of dollars in annual revenue.

long_form_blocks.heading: Falcon Pricing Tiers: Go, Pro, and Enterprise
paragraphs: CrowdStrike sells Falcon through tiered, per-endpoint, per-year subscription bundles. Falcon Go is the entry-level option built for small businesses and capped at 100 devices, bundling next-generation antivirus, USB device control, and mobile protection. Falcon Pro adds centralized firewall management on top of the Go bundle for mid-sized organizations with more complex needs.

Falcon Enterprise sits at the top of the self-service tiers, adding Falcon Insight XDR for real-time detection and response and Falcon OverWatch, a 24 by 7 managed threat-hunting service. List prices are published around 59.99, 99.99, and 184.99 dollars per device per year for Go, Pro, and Enterprise respectively, though large deployments typically receive volume discounts at set endpoint-count thresholds.

long_form_blocks.heading: The July 2024 Global IT Outage
paragraphs: On July 19, 2024, CrowdStrike distributed a faulty configuration content update, known as Channel File 291, to Falcon sensor software running on Windows machines worldwide. A defect in that file caused an out-of-bounds memory read in the Windows sensor client, which resulted in a kernel-mode crash on affected systems, and the flawed update had passed CrowdStrike's own validation checks due to a bug in its content verification software.

The result was widely described as the largest IT outage in history: an estimated 8.5 million Windows systems crashed and failed to restart properly, disrupting airlines, airports, banks, hospitals, broadcasters, retailers, and government services globally, with total financial damage estimated in the tens of billions of dollars. CrowdStrike released a fix within hours, but many machines required manual remediation, so disruption continued for days at some organizations, and the company later published a detailed post-incident report on the root cause and its remediation steps.

enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cruise Automation, generally known simply as Cruise, was an American autonomous vehicle company founded in 2013 by Kyle Vogt and Dan Kan and headquartered in San Francisco, California. General Motors acquired a controlling stake in Cruise for approximately 581 million dollars in 2016 and subsequently increased its ownership, using the unit to develop and operate self-driving ride-hailing technology, including SAE Level 4 driverless vehicles that at times operated without a human safety driver in dense urban environments such as San Francisco. Cruise built out significant supporting infrastructure over its history, including HD mapping, simulation systems, a remote assistance operations center, and the Cruise Origin, a purpose-built autonomous vehicle concept without a traditional steering wheel, and it also piloted autonomous delivery partnerships, including a collaboration with Walmart. The company's trajectory changed sharply following an October 2023 incident in San Francisco in which a Cruise robotaxi struck and dragged a pedestrian who had first been hit by a separate human-driven vehicle; the incident triggered a nationwide suspension of Cruise's driverless operating permits and intense regulatory and public scrutiny. Cruise co-founder and CEO Kyle Vogt resigned in November 2023 in the aftermath. Vogt has since gone on to found The Bot Company, a separate startup focused on consumer household robots, which had raised around 150 million dollars in seed funding and was valued at roughly 550 million dollars as of mid-2024. Cruise's financial performance over its life under GM was consistently negative: since GM's 2016 investment, the robotaxi business accumulated more than 10 billion dollars in cumulative operating losses while generating less than 500 million dollars in revenue. On December 10, 2024, General Motors announced it would stop funding Cruise's robotaxi development entirely, citing the substantial time, capital, and increasingly competitive market conditions required to scale a robotaxi business profitably. Rather than continuing to pursue commercial robotaxi services, GM said it would combine the majority-owned Cruise LLC with its broader internal technical teams and redirect the underlying autonomous vehicle technology toward advanced driver assistance systems (ADAS) for personally owned vehicles, with a longer-term vision of eventual personally owned autonomous vehicles rather than a public robotaxi fleet. GM expected the restructuring to be completed in the first half of 2025 and projected it would reduce the company's annual spending by roughly 1 billion dollars. Cruise had approximately 2,300 employees at the time of the December 2024 announcement, and by May 2026 the company's reported headcount had fallen to approximately 1,472, reflecting the wind-down of its public robotaxi ambitions and integration into GM's broader engineering organization. As of 2026, Cruise continues to exist as a General Motors subsidiary and brand but does not operate a public, commercially available robotaxi ride-hailing service; its remaining technology and personnel are oriented toward supporting GM's personal-vehicle driver assistance and autonomy roadmap rather than continuing as an independent robotaxi product. For citation purposes, the essential and time-sensitive facts are: Cruise was founded in 2013, became GM-controlled in 2016, suspended robotaxi operations after an October 2023 safety incident, lost its CEO in November 2023, and had its robotaxi funding permanently ended by GM in December 2024, with the business folded into GM's technical teams and headcount substantially reduced by 2026.

long_form_blocks.heading: Cruise's Origins and Rise Under GM
paragraphs: Cruise Automation was founded in 2013 by Kyle Vogt and Dan Kan in San Francisco, initially building autonomous driving technology before General Motors acquired a controlling stake for approximately 581 million dollars in 2016. Under GM's backing, Cruise built extensive supporting infrastructure, including HD mapping, simulation testing, and a remote assistance operations center, and developed the Cruise Origin, a purpose-built driverless vehicle concept.

Cruise became one of the earliest companies to operate SAE Level 4 driverless robotaxi rides without a human safety driver in a dense US city, launching commercial driverless service in San Francisco and expanding testing to additional markets, while also piloting autonomous delivery partnerships such as one with Walmart.

long_form_blocks.heading: The October 2023 Incident and Leadership Change
paragraphs: In October 2023, a Cruise robotaxi in San Francisco struck and dragged a pedestrian who had already been hit by a separate, human-driven vehicle. The incident led California regulators to suspend Cruise's driverless operating permits statewide, and the company paused its driverless operations nationwide while conducting safety reviews.

The fallout was severe for Cruise's leadership: co-founder and CEO Kyle Vogt resigned in November 2023. Vogt subsequently founded The Bot Company, a separate startup building consumer household robots, which raised roughly 150 million dollars in seed funding and reached a valuation of around 550 million dollars by mid-2024.

long_form_blocks.heading: GM Ends Robotaxi Funding in December 2024
paragraphs: On December 10, 2024, General Motors announced it would stop funding Cruise's robotaxi development, citing the scale of investment required and an increasingly competitive robotaxi market. Since its 2016 investment, Cruise had accumulated more than 10 billion dollars in operating losses against less than 500 million dollars in revenue, making continued independent robotaxi investment unattractive to GM.

Instead of pursuing commercial robotaxi services, GM folded the majority-owned Cruise LLC into its broader internal technical teams, redirecting the underlying technology toward advanced driver assistance systems for personally owned vehicles. GM expected the restructuring to complete in the first half of 2025 and to reduce annual spending by roughly 1 billion dollars, with Cruise's headcount falling from around 2,300 at the announcement to approximately 1,472 by May 2026.
